subject: ap-psychology
title: Research Methods — Experimental Design, Statistics, and Memory
stimulus:
Mr. Gomez decides to conduct a study with his sixth-grade math class, after first obtaining informed consent. Half of his students happen to arrive early for class (group 1), so he uses the time to give them some extra problem-solving tips. The next week he compares their scores on a quiz with the scores of the students who arrived on time (group 2) and did not receive the tips. The students’ grades are represented in the table below. Mr. Gomez comes to the conclusion that the problem-solving tips led to higher scores on the quiz. Mr. Gomez posts the table of grades on the door of his classroom to emphasize that the tips helped improve the student grades.

| Group 1 | Grade on Quiz | Group 2 | Grade on Quiz |
|---------|---------------|---------|---------------|
| Jaime | 4 | Lynda | 3 |
| Steven | 5 | Adam | 4 |
| Thomas | 3 | Sami | 1 |
| Elizabeth | 3 | Marlena | 1 |
| Marwa | 7 | Kiara | 5 |
| Frances | 6 | Caylin | 4 |
| Fekru | 3 | Darin | 2 |
| David | 6 | Chinami | 4 |

parts.prompt: Identify the dependent variable presented in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate score on the quiz as the dependent variable.

required_elements: The dependent variable is the score the students earn on the quiz.
alternatives.correct_example: The dependent variable is the score the students earn on the quiz.

wrong_examples: The dependent variable is the one that is measured.
The dependent variable is the problem-solving tips.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify the score on the quiz (or quiz grade/quiz performance) as the dependent variable. The dependent variable is what is measured in the study. Naming a general process (e.g., 'the variable that is measured') without specifying quiz score does not earn the point. Naming the problem-solving tips (the independent variable) does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the score the students earn on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies quiz score as the measured outcome variable.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the quiz grade.
sample_responses.commentary: Quiz grade is an acceptable equivalent phrasing for score on the quiz.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the one that is measured.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes DV in general terms without specifying what is actually measured (quiz scores); too vague to earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The dependent variable is the problem-solving tips.
sample_responses.commentary: The problem-solving tips are the independent variable (what was manipulated), not the dependent variable.

parts.prompt: Explain how the study could be modified to be an experiment.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the study could be modified by adding random  assignment to make it an experiment.

required_elements: The study needs to have random assignment in each experimental condition in order for it to be an experiment.
alternatives.correct_example: The study needs to have random assignment in each experimental condition in order for it to be an experiment.

required_elements: Mr. Gomez would need to put people in groups randomly in order to make this an experiment.
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Gomez would need to put people in groups randomly in order to make this an experiment.

wrong_examples: Mr. Gomez manipulated his variable, which makes it an experiment.
Mr. Gomez might avoid bias by randomly selecting people for his study.
Mr. Gomez would have to make sure that both groups received instructions for the
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must specify that random assignment — randomly assigning participants to the experimental conditions (tip group vs. no-tip group) — is what would make this study a true experiment. Random selection (randomly choosing participants from a population) does not earn the point because the study already has all students from the class. Simply stating that the groups should be equal or that Mr. Gomez should give instructions to both groups does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: The study needs to have random assignment in each experimental condition in order for it to be an experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies random assignment as the modification needed to make this a true experiment.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez would need to put people in groups randomly in order to make this an experiment.
sample_responses.commentary: Equivalent phrasing — randomly placing students into the two groups is random assignment.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez might avoid bias by randomly selecting people for his study.
sample_responses.commentary: Confuses random selection (sampling method) with random assignment (group allocation). The study uses Mr. Gomez's own class, so random selection is not the issue; random assignment to conditions is required.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez would have to make sure that both groups received instructions for the problem-solving tips.
sample_responses.commentary: Describes giving the treatment to all participants, which would eliminate the control group — this does not fix the lack of random assignment.

parts.prompt: Compare the mode of group 1 to the mode of group 2.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate either that the mode of Group 1 is lower than the mode of  Group 2 (or vice versa). 

OR 

The response must indicate that the mode of Group 1 is 3, and the mode of Group 2 is 4.

required_elements: The mode of group 1 is lower than group 2.
alternatives.correct_example: The mode of group 1 is lower than group 2.

required_elements: Group 1’s mode is 3 and Group 2’s mode is 4, so they are different.
alternatives.correct_example: Group 1’s mode is 3 and Group 2’s mode is 4, so they are different.

scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must compare the modes of the two groups. Group 1 has scores 4, 5, 3, 3, 7, 6, 3, 6 — the mode is 3 (appears three times). Group 2 has scores 3, 4, 1, 1, 5, 4, 2, 4 — the mode is 4 (appears three times). An acceptable response either states the numeric values of both modes (3 and 4) or states a directional comparison (Group 1’s mode is lower than Group 2’s mode, or Group 2’s mode is higher than Group 1’s). Simply stating one mode without comparing, or computing the mean instead of the mode, does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Group 1’s mode is 3 and Group 2’s mode is 4, so they are different.
sample_responses.commentary: Provides the correct numeric values of both modes and explicitly compares them.

sample_responses.response: The mode of Group 1 is lower than the mode of Group 2.
sample_responses.commentary: A directional comparison without numeric values is acceptable as long as the direction is correct (Group 1 mode < Group 2 mode).

sample_responses.response: Group 1’s mode is 3.
sample_responses.commentary: Identifies one group’s mode but does not compare it to Group 2’s mode; the prompt requires a comparison.

parts.prompt: Identify the measure of central tendency that needs to be calculated to determine the standard deviation.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that the mean is the measure of central tendency that is  calculated to determine the standard deviation.

required_elements: Mr. Gomez must calculate the mean in order to determine the standard deviation.
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Gomez must calculate the mean in order to determine the standard deviation.

wrong_examples: Mr. Gomez must calculate the mode to determine the standard deviation.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify the mean (arithmetic average) as the measure of central tendency needed to compute standard deviation. Standard deviation measures how spread out scores are from the mean; you cannot calculate SD without first finding the mean. The median and the mode are not used in standard deviation calculations. Simply saying 'average' without specifying 'mean' is generally acceptable.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez must calculate the mean in order to determine the standard deviation.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly identifies the mean and explains its role as a prerequisite for computing standard deviation.

sample_responses.response: The mean.
sample_responses.commentary: A single-word/phrase answer naming the mean earns the point — no elaboration required.

sample_responses.response: The median.
sample_responses.commentary: The median is not used in computing standard deviation; standard deviation requires the mean.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez must calculate the mode to determine the standard deviation.
sample_responses.commentary: The mode (most frequent value) is not used in standard deviation calculations.

parts.prompt: Explain the ethical flaw that is explicitly presented in the study.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that Mr. Gomez posted the students’ grade (or did not  maintain confidentiality/anonymity of the students’ grades).

required_elements: Mr. Gomez posted every kid’s name with their score.
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Gomez posted every kid’s name with their score.

wrong_examples: Mr. Gomez did harm to his students in this study.
scoring_points.official_rubric: The response must identify the specific ethical violation that is explicitly described in the stimulus: Mr. Gomez posted the table of grades (with student names) on the classroom door, violating confidentiality or anonymity. A response that mentions general harm without identifying the confidentiality breach does not earn the point. The ethical issue must be tied to the act described in the scenario (posting names with scores).

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez posted every kid’s name with their score on the classroom door, which violates the confidentiality of the students.
sample_responses.commentary: Directly identifies the act described in the scenario (posting names with grades) and correctly labels it as a confidentiality violation.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez violated students’ anonymity by publicly displaying individual scores next to their names.
sample_responses.commentary: Anonymity (no one should be able to link a score to a student) is violated when names are paired with scores publicly.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez did harm to his students in this study.
sample_responses.commentary: States harm in general terms without identifying the specific ethical breach (posting names and grades publicly); too vague to earn the point.

parts.prompt: Explain how metacognition could apply to the scenario.

scoring_points.description: The response must indicate that an individual in the scenario thinks about their own  thinking.

required_elements: Students in this study might keep track of how well they do, so they can study better for each test.
alternatives.correct_example: Students in this study might keep track of how well they do, so they can study better for each test.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Metacognition is thinking about one's own thinking or cognitive processes. The response must apply this concept to a specific person in the scenario (students or Mr. Gomez) by describing an instance where they monitor, evaluate, or regulate their own thinking or learning strategies. Simply defining metacognition without applying it to the scenario does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Students in this study might keep track of how well they do on quizzes, so they can adjust how they study for each test.
sample_responses.commentary: Shows a student monitoring their own performance (thinking about their learning) and using that reflection to adjust strategies — a clear application of metacognition.

sample_responses.response: Mr. Gomez is thinking about how his teaching method (the problem-solving tips) affects student performance, and then reflecting on whether to use it again.
sample_responses.commentary: Teacher reflection on instructional effectiveness counts as metacognition — thinking about one's own thinking/teaching process.

sample_responses.response: Metacognition means thinking about your own thinking.
sample_responses.commentary: Defines the term without applying it to anyone in the scenario; must connect the concept to Mr. Gomez's students or the study.

parts.prompt: Suppose Mr. Gomez had conducted this study as an experiment without any flaws and obtained the same results and that the results were statistically significant. Explain how the findings depicted in the table above could support the theory of levels of processing.

scoring_points.description:
The response must indicate either that Mr. Gomez used problem-solving tips that  promoted deep processing and increased scores. 

OR 

The response must indicate that the group that did not receive the tips used shallow  processing and scored worse.

required_elements: Students who used the problem-solving strategies used deep processing and that helped them learn more in class.
alternatives.correct_example: Students who used the problem-solving strategies used deep processing and that helped them learn more in class.

required_elements: Mr. Gomez’s strategies worked well because they helped students process things
alternatives.correct_example: Mr. Gomez’s strategies worked well because they helped students process things

required_elements: more deeply. Students who did not use the problem-solving strategies used shallow processing and learned less in class.
alternatives.correct_example: more deeply. Students who did not use the problem-solving strategies used shallow processing and learned less in class.

scoring_points.official_rubric: Levels of processing theory (Craik & Lockhart) holds that deeper, more meaningful processing leads to better memory and learning outcomes than shallow (surface-level) processing. The response must link the problem-solving tips to deep processing AND connect that to higher quiz scores, OR link the absence of tips to shallow processing AND connect that to lower scores. A response that merely names the theory without applying either deep or shallow processing to the groups in the scenario does not earn the point.

sample_responses.response: Students who used the problem-solving strategies engaged in deep processing of the material, which led to better retention and higher quiz scores.
sample_responses.commentary: Correctly links the tips (treatment) to deep processing and ties deep processing to improved quiz performance.

sample_responses.response: Students who did not use the problem-solving strategies used shallow processing and learned less, which is why they scored worse on the quiz.
sample_responses.commentary: Applies levels of processing from the control group angle — lack of tips leads to shallow processing and lower scores.

sample_responses.response: Levels of processing theory says deeper processing leads to better memory.
sample_responses.commentary: Defines the theory without applying deep or shallow processing to the specific groups in the scenario.
